Preface
The Principles of Accounts Teaching Module for Form 1 Term 2 has been developed to 
provide a continuation from the Principles of Accounts Teaching Module for Form 1 Term 1. 
This Module has been designed to support teachers to effectively deliver Principles of Accounts 
lessons under the new curriculum. The module aims to bridge the gap created by the absence of 
approved textbooks and to equip teachers with practical activities, and assessments tailored to 
the learners’ level and needs.
The Ministry understands the challenges that come with transitioning to a new curriculum 
framework. This module reflects the commitment to making this journey as smooth as possible 
for teachers, by providing structured guidance, suggested activities, and assessments that align 
with the CBC’s objectives. However, we also encourage teachers to supplement this module with 
their own research and innovations to address any gaps that may arise during implementation.
The suggested activities and assessments provided in this module are designed to foster practical 
and critical thinking skills in learners while ensuring relevance and adaptability to different 
school contexts. Teachers are encouraged to tailor these suggestions to their unique classroom 
dynamics and to explore alternative, acceptable approaches when necessary. 
We hope this module will serve as a valuable resource for teachers as they implement the new 
Competence-Based Curriculum, enhancing their ability to inspire and equip learners with 
knowledge, skills and values.
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD) 
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

INTRODUCTION 
The Principles of Accounts Teaching Module for Form One Term 2 comprise content that has 
been sequenced to create a link with the Principles of Accounts Teaching Module for Term 1. 
The suggested learning activities are designed in such a way as to promote cognitive and 
technical skills of the learners. You should therefore ensure that learners are actively engaged in 
the learning activities as stipulated in the module for masterly of the competences. 
The module provides:
 Proposals of learner centred teaching methodology.  However, you are free to explore 
other learner centred methodologies to suit your local environment as well as available 
resources.
 An example of how a learning activity can be conducted to support Competence-Based 
Curriculum.
 Content Tips to provide knowledge for the learning activity.  
 Examples of class exercises and homework assignments suitable for Competence Based 
Curriculum

1.5  TOPIC: BOOKS OF PRIME ENTRY
Introduction
Books of Prime Entry are Books of Accounts used for recording different types of Business 
Transactions from the various source documents. These books are also called Subsidiary Books, 
Day Books, Journals or Books of Original Entry. 
Books of Prime Entry are important because they provide accurate records, help in financial re­
porting and enable financial analysis.
General Competences
 
 Analytical Thinking
 
 Communication 
 
 Critical Thinking
 
 Entrepreneurship
 
 Financial Education
 
 Problem Solving
1.5.1 Sub – Topic – Types of Books of Prime Entry
Introduction
Books of Prime Entry are Books of Accounts used for recording different types of Business 
Transactions from the various source documents received or issued by the business when 
a transaction takes place, before posting to the Ledger. They include Purchases Day Book, 
Purchases Returns Day Book, Sales Day Book, Sales Returns Day Book, Cash Book (One 
Column Cash Book, Two Column Cash Book and Three Column Cash Book), Petty Cash Book 
and the General Journal.  Each Book of Prime Entry records a group of similar transactions.   
The following are the types of the Books of Prime Entry and their corresponding transactions:

Note: Types of Books of Prime Entry as a sub topic should be taught by introducing each type of 
Book of Prime Entry with the activities stated in the syllabus. However, as the sub topic is being 
introduced, a general introduction of each Book of Prime Entry must be done. 
Specific Competence 
 
 Record Business Transactions in the Books of Prime Entry 
 
 Post Business Transactions to the Ledger
KEY TERMS
 
 Books of Prime Entry – books in which business transactions are first recorded.
 
 Source Documents – these are documents that show proof of a transaction.
 
 Purchases – Purchases refer to the acquisition of goods, services or assets by a firm 
for resale 
 
 Trade Discount – The reduction in the price of items on the gross invoice price or list 
price for bulky buying.
 
 Credit Purchases – these are goods bought on credit from suppliers for resale.

 Explaining Books of Prime Entry 
 
 Stating the Source Documents for each Book of Prime Entry.

CONTENT TIPS
Source Documents
Source Documents are written or printed records, which provide proof of a Business Transaction. 
Books of Prime Entry are prepared using source documents. They provide details for which 
Books of Prime Entry are to be prepared. The design of each document differs from one Business 
to the other, but all of them will contain similar information. 
Importance of Source Documents
a)	 They provide proof of occurrence of a transaction.
b)	 They ensure correct and accurate details of a business transaction.
c)	 They provide reference for entries of transactions into the Books of Prime Entry.
d)	 They help in resolving disputes related to payments.

Discounts 
It is the reduction in the price of goods or services.  Discounts can be offered for various reasons 
such as for bulky buying and quick payment of debts.
The following are the types of discounts that will be used in the preparations of the Books of 
Prime Entry:
a)	 Trade Discount
It is the reduction in the list price of goods by a supplier when goods/services are 
sold to another for bulky buying and/or profit margin.  It is normally a fixed amount 
or percentage deducted from the list price.  Trade discount is used when calculating 
the net invoice price and as such, does not enter the accounting records.
The importance of trade discount to the supplier (seller) 
 
 To encourage customers to buy in large quantities and well as make repeated 
purchases.
 
 To overcome competition from competing suppliers.
Importance to a business customer (buyer)
 
 To enable to resale their goods at competitive prices
b)	 Cash Discount
A cash discount is a reduction in the amount due on an invoice or payment.  It is a 
reduction in price for prompt payment of debts. 
There are two types of cash discounts:
i)	 Discount Allowed – a type of cash discount allowed by a business to its customers 
when they pay their accounts promptly.
ii)	 Discount Received – a type of cash discount received by a business from its 
suppliers  when it pays what it owes them promptly.

Task 1
Mulu Wholesaler has been in business for the past five years dealing in second hand clothes, 
which are sold to several customers in bulk.  On 3rd October 2024, the Wholesaler received 
a complaint from one of its customers who did not understand the amount indicated on an 
Invoice because of the Trade Discount charged. As a Wholesaler, you decided to send one 
of your workers to visit the customer concerned to clearly demonstrate the calculation 
of Trade Discount and amount to be paid based on the following item indicated on the 
Invoice earlier sent to the customer:   
20 bales of Golf T-Shirts at a cost price of K 8, 000.00 per bale, with an allowance of 10% 
Trade Discount. 
Solution
Step 1:	
Calculation of the total amount before Trade Discount.
                                 = 20 X K8, 000.00
                                 = K160,000.00
Step 2: Calculation of Trade Discount
Trade Discount = Gross Invoice Price x Trade Discount 
                           = K160,000.00 X 10
                                                       100
                           = K16,000.00  Trade Discount
Step 3: Calculation of how much the retailer will pay after a 10% Trade Discount
Net Invoice Price 	
= Gross Invoice Price – Trade Discount
                 	
	
= K160,000.00 – K16, 000.00
     	
 	
= K144 000.00  
Task 2
Nchimunya a retailer, has been buying goods in large quantity from Chalwe General Dealers 
who allows both Trade and Cash Discounts to be charged on the list price.  The charging of 
Discounts to the list price has caused challenges to Nchimunya in understanding how the Supplier 
determines the amount to be paid.  Based on the 10 Dresses bought at K200.00 each on 10 April, 
2025 with 10% Trade Discount and a further  Cash Discount of 5% if payment is made within two 
weeks.  Now that payment was made within the credit period, as a Business, you have requested 
a qualified Person to calculate Trade and Cash Discounts in order to determine the amount paid.
Solutions 
	
Step 1: Calculation of Cost of Dresses:
10 Dresses X K200.00 
= K2,000.00

Principles of Accounts

Step 2: Calculation of Trade Discount:
K2,000.00 X 10   
                     100
= K200.00 Trade Discount
Step 3: Calculation of Cash Discount
	
Cash Discount = (Gross Invoice Price - Trade Discount) X  Cash Discount
	
	
=  (K2,000 – K200) X 5   
	
                                               100
	
	
= K1, 800 X 5   
        	 	
       100
 	
	
= K90.00 Cash Discount 
	
Step 4: Calculation of Amount Paid:
Amount to be paid 	
=  Net Invoice Price – Cash Discount
            	
	
	
=  K1,800 – K90
           		
	
   	
=  K1,710.00

1.5.2 Sub – Topic –Types of Books of Prime Entry – Purchases Day Book
Introduction
The Purchases Day Book is one of the Books of Prime Entry where we record all goods bought 
on credit and are meant for resale.  It is also known as a Purchases Journal, Creditors or Bought 
Journal.  The source document used to write up the Purchases Day Book is known as the Original 
Invoice
Specific Competences  
 
 Record Business Transactions in the Books of Prime Entry – Purchases Day Book 
 
 Post Business Transactions to the Ledger
KEY TERMS
 
 Purchases – Purchases refer to the acquisition of goods for resale.
 
 Credit Purchases – These are goods bought on credit from suppliers for resale. 
 
 Posting – Making a second entry in the ledger in order to complete the double entry 
system.
 
 Trial Balance – It is a list of balances extracted from the Ledger.

1.5.3 Sub-Topic:  Types of Books of Prime Entry - Purchases Returns Day Book
Introduction 
The Purchases Returns Day Book is also known as the Returns Outwards Journal or Returns 
Outward Day Book or Purchases Returns Journal. This is a Book of Prime Entry where goods 
that were once bought but later returned due to various reasons such as wrong colour, wrong 
order, wrong sizes etc., are recorded. This book is important because it contains a record of pur­
chases returned (goods returned to suppliers). This book is prepared from the source document 
known as the Original Credit Note. 
Specific Competence
 
 Record Business Transactions in the Books of Prime Entry - Purchases Returns 
Day Book.  
 
 Post Business Transactions to the Ledger
KEY TERMS
 
 Purchases Returns – Goods that were bought and now returned to the Seller.
